title: US Weapons Stockpiles Strained, F-35 Readiness Hits Record Low, and Europe's Fighter Future Splinters
subhead: US military readiness faces compounding pressures as depleted weapon stockpiles meet an F-35 fleet where only 25% are fully mission capable, while Germany and Spain forge ahead on a new sixth-generation fighter after FCAS collapses and NATO's Ankara summit signals a fundamental burden-shifting in alliance defense.

sections.heading: US Weapon Stockpiles Depleted as Iran Conflict Heats Up
sections.body:
Key US weapons stockpiles remain significantly depleted and will come under even more intense pressure if strikes against Iran continue, CNN reports. The warning adds a procurement dimension to what has primarily been framed as a geopolitical crisis: the US-Iran ceasefire framework signed in June has not stopped intermittent military exchanges, and each new escalation draws on munitions already stretched thin from years of transfers to Ukraine and maintaining readiness in the Indo-Pacific.

The depletion affects precision-guided munitions, air defense interceptors, and naval strike weapons across multiple categories. Pentagon planners have flagged the gap repeatedly, but industrial base capacity limits how quickly production can scale. The problem is not new — it has been accumulating since 2022 — but the Iran conflict has turned a slow-burn industrial concern into an acute readiness issue.

Poland's recent transfer of just five PAC-3 MSE interceptor missiles to Ukraine from its own stock of 208 underscores the severity: allies are rationing single-digit quantities of critical munitions that were once shipped in pallets.
sections.whyItMatters: The US is now juggling three active theaters demanding the same categories of precision munitions — Europe, the Middle East, and the Indo-Pacific — with an industrial base that cannot replenish fast enough. This is no longer a future risk; it is a present constraint on operational planning.

sections.heading: Only 25% of F-35 Fleet Fully Mission Capable as Readiness Slides
sections.body:
A scathing Government Accountability Office report found that only 25% of US F-35s were fully mission capable in fiscal year 2025, down from 38% in fiscal 2021. The broader mission capable rate fell from 67% to 44% over the same period. The US now operates more than 800 F-35s, with plans to acquire about 1,700 more by the mid-2040s — but the fleet's sustainment system is falling behind the aircraft's growth.

GAO identified multiple causes: software delays that forced the military to accept non-mission-capable aircraft, spare parts shortages across 48 critical components including canopies, corrosion inspection and repair backlogs, and an industrial base that cannot yet deliver the $7 billion in additional parts the reset plan requires. Engine contractor officials warned material shortages could persist through 2029.

The F-35 Joint Program Office launched a Global Support Solution Reset in June 2025, requesting $13.7 billion through fiscal 2031 to reach 80% mission capable and 65% full mission capable rates by 2030. But GAO warned improvements may not appear until late 2026, and conditions could worsen before they improve.

GAO also criticized contractor incentives: the Pentagon paid Lockheed Martin over $114 million out of $269 million in available incentive fees from 2020-2023 even as readiness rates stagnated or declined. Of 46 GAO recommendations on F-35 sustainment since 2014, DOD has fully implemented only 14.
sections.whyItMatters: The F-35 is the centerpiece of US and allied air power across three services and a dozen partner nations. A fleet where three out of four jets cannot perform all assigned missions is not a sustainment problem — it is an operational readiness crisis that directly constrains US warfighting capacity in every theater.

sections.heading: Germany and Spain Launch 'Team Gen 6' After FCAS Fighter Program Collapses
sections.body:
Germany and Spain are forming a new coalition called 'Team Gen 6' to develop sixth-generation fighter technology after the Franco-German Future Combat Air System program collapsed over what The War Zone described as irreconcilable differences on requirements. The breakup ends years of effort to create a European rival to the US NGAD program and the UK-led Tempest/GCAS project.

FCAS had been the flagship of European defense collaboration, bringing together Dassault, Airbus, and Indra to build a next-generation fighter and accompanying combat cloud. The split leaves France without its primary next-gen fighter partner and raises questions about whether Europe can field competitive sixth-generation platforms without consolidating its fragmented aerospace industry.

Germany and Spain's move to proceed independently signals that Berlin has decided French participation is no longer a prerequisite for its future air power — a significant strategic calculation that will reshape European defense industrial partnerships for decades.
sections.whyItMatters: The collapse of FCAS is the most consequential defense-industrial rupture in Europe in a generation. It fragments European fighter development into at least three tracks (Team Gen 6, French-led efforts, UK Tempest) at a time when the US and China are advancing their own programs, and it raises hard questions about whether European defense integration can survive the competing industrial interests of its major powers.

sections.heading: US Marines Plan Permanent War-Ready Weapons Stockpile in Australia
sections.body:
US Navy tender documents reveal advanced planning for a permanent Marine Corps weapons stockpile in Australia's southeast, with $30 million allocated to build warehouses and offices in Victoria state. The stockpile — a first for the Marine Corps in Australia — will be positioned in Melbourne before moving to US-built warehouses at Bandiana military base in rural Victoria, with expected full capacity by 2028.

The facility will store 'crew-served weapons' and employ around 110 engineers, mechanics, and material specialists managed by a global defense contractor. The Pentagon has separately requested $500 million from Congress for fiscal 2027 to improve prepositioning of equipment and fuel across the Indo-Pacific.

The placement in Australia's southeast is deliberate. A Lowy Institute report this week warned that China can strike northern Australia with ballistic missiles from South China Sea outposts. Analyst Sam Roggeveen told AFP the southeast location was likely 'a relevant consideration,' while warning the facilities would become 'obvious targets' once operational. ANU professor John Blaxland noted Australia's location is being seen with 'a growing sense of significance' given concerns about the vulnerability of Guam.

A separate first land stockpile in the Asia-Pacific is expected to open this year in the Philippines, close to potential South China Sea flashpoints.
sections.whyItMatters: This is the physical infrastructure of deterrence in the Indo-Pacific. Two prepositioning sites — Philippines and Australia — within 12 months signals that the US is building logistics depth for a potential Taiwan contingency, moving beyond rotational troop presence to sustainment-capable forward positioning.

sections.heading: Ukraine Seeks US License to Produce Anti-Ballistic Missiles Domestically
sections.body:
President Zelensky said Ukraine is requesting US approval to manufacture anti-ballistic missile systems and interceptors on Ukrainian soil, following a direct conversation with Trump on the sidelines of the G7 summit in France. 'God willing, this time we will succeed in obtaining licenses to produce the relevant anti-ballistic systems and missiles,' Zelensky said, without specifying which systems were under discussion.

The request comes as Ukraine faces a severe Patriot interceptor shortage. Multiple reports confirm that by early July 2026, Ukraine's Patriot PAC-3 interceptor supply had reached critical lows — the only air defense system currently capable of intercepting Russian Iskander-M ballistic missiles. Poland's transfer of just five PAC-3 MSE missiles to Ukraine from its own stockpile of 208 illustrates the global scarcity of these interceptors.

Trump reportedly responded positively to requests for additional Patriot missiles but did not publicly confirm a final decision on production licensing. Zelensky noted the core challenge: 'Production isn't as high as our needs. Production takes place in the US.' Rep. Michael McCaul endorsed the idea, telling media that Ukraine could produce Patriot missiles 'more efficiently' if licensed.

The NYT reported that a license to produce Patriot systems in Ukraine would be a significant boost, but could take years to translate into operational capacity — requiring technology transfer, facility construction, and workforce training.
sections.whyItMatters: If approved, this would be the first time the US licenses Patriot production outside its current manufacturing base. It would mark a fundamental shift in how the US approaches arms transfer — from shipping finite stockpiles to enabling allied production — and could establish Ukraine as a future missile manufacturing hub for European air defense.

sections.heading: India-UAE BrahMos Missile Negotiations Reshape Gulf Defense Procurement
sections.body:
India is in preliminary talks to sell the BrahMos Mach 2.8 supersonic cruise missile and the Akashteer air defense command system to the United Arab Emirates, according to Reuters. No deal has been announced, but four Indian sources confirmed discussions are underway, with two confirming Akashteer is part of the package.

The potential sale reflects Abu Dhabi's drive to diversify its defense suppliers beyond the Western arms that currently dominate: SIPRI data shows the US accounts for 54% of Middle East arms imports from 2021-2025, followed by Italy at 12% and France at 11%. The UAE already operates THAAD and Patriot systems and has a defense cooperation memorandum with South Korea worth over $35 billion.

For India, the deal would advance its defense export ambitions. India's defense exports reached $4.11 billion in the fiscal year ending March 2026, and BrahMos has already been sold to the Philippines with deals signed with Vietnam and Indonesia. But BrahMos requires Russian consent for third-party sales because it is a joint India-Russia system — a complication that could become a geopolitical lever.

Indian government sources told Reuters that closer India-UAE defense ties are partly a counterweight to the Saudi-Pakistan defense pact, adding a regional rivalry dimension to what might look like a straightforward procurement decision.
sections.whyItMatters: A BrahMos sale to the UAE would be India's most consequential defense export deal and a milestone for non-Western arms suppliers in the Gulf. It would also test whether a joint India-Russia weapon system can navigate Great Power consent requirements in a region where all three powers have competing interests.

sections.heading: France Shuts Down Israeli Stands at Eurosatory, Sparking Diplomatic Row
sections.body:
French authorities shut down a dozen Israeli company stands at the Eurosatory 2026 defense exhibition outside Paris, according to Barrons and Al-Monitor. The closures sparked outrage from Israeli officials and highlighted the growing tension between arms export commerce and political pressure over Israel's military operations.

Eurosatory 2026, described as the largest defense exhibition in its history by Tech Times, nonetheless became a focal point for the politicization of defense trade. France also used the show to announce procurement of its own: a Saab NLAW contract signed by the DGA, and an order for Origin Robotics Blaze interceptor UAVs for counter-drone operations.

India displayed its Pinaka guided rocket system and RudraM missiles at the show, with Prime Minister Modi publicly backing joint production with European partners — a signal of India's expanding defense export strategy.
sections.whyItMatters: The Israeli stand closures at the world's largest land defense expo mark a new threshold in the politicization of defense trade. When a host nation shuts down exhibitors from a major defense exporter at the industry's premier event, it signals that political considerations are now overriding the traditional commercial neutrality of defense exhibitions — a precedent that could reshape how defense trade shows operate globally.

bottomLine: This week's defense landscape is defined by the strain between ambition and capacity. The US F-35 fleet — the most expensive weapon program in history at $1.6 trillion — can only field a quarter of its jets fully mission-capable, while depleted munitions stockpiles face fresh demand from the Iran conflict. Europe's fighter future has fractured with FCAS's collapse, and the NATO Ankara summit formalized a burden-shifting that will redefine alliance defense spending regardless of who occupies the White House. Meanwhile, new procurement patterns are emerging: India negotiating BrahMos sales to the UAE, Taiwan firing HIMARS toward China, and Ukraine seeking to produce Patriot missiles under license. The common thread is that established defense supply chains and industrial partnerships are being tested — and in many cases, reconfigured — faster than the institutions managing them can adapt.
sources: GAO (F-35 report gao-26-108113), CNN (US weapon stockpiles depleted), AFP (US Marines stockpile Australia), Reuters (India-UAE BrahMos talks), Kyiv Post (Zelensky anti-ballistic missile license), New York Times (Patriot production license), SCMP (China stealth coating, US stockpile Australia), The War Zone (FCAS collapse, Team Gen 6, Russia Arctic infrastructure), Barrons/Al-Monitor (Eurosatory Israeli stands), Tech Times (Eurosatory 2026 largest show), Janes (Eurosatory NLAW, Blaze UAV orders), Jerusalem Post (India Meprolight MEPRO X6), Anadolu Agency (Sudan drone strikes), AP (Taiwan HIMARS), UPI (North Korea nuclear irreversible), Voz Populi (F-35 GAO analysis, BrahMos-UAE), Lowy Institute (China missile range Australia), Firstpost (NATO Ankara summit analysis), Poland defense press (Patriot transfer), SIPRI (Middle East arms imports data)
